summary: DHH recounts his Xbox 360 breaking down with the 'red ring of death' just days after Microsoft announced a billion-dollar warranty extension program. Despite the massive investment, he found the actual service experience terrible: long hold times, slow shipping, and a 4-6 week repair timeline. He contrasts this unfavorably with Apple's overnight delivery approach. The post uses his personal frustration to make a broader point about how throwing money at a problem doesn't guarantee good customer service.
key_points: Microsoft's billion-dollar warranty program delivered a poor actual customer experience with hour-long hold times and slow shipping
The contrast between the headline investment and the ground-level service reality highlights a disconnect between spending and execution
Apple's overnight delivery is cited as the standard Microsoft should have matched
A two-month turnaround for a known, widespread hardware defect is unacceptable regardless of budget
money_quotes: Now you'd think a billion dollars would buy Microsoft some premium, grade-A service and expedition to make short order of such a widespread problem, no? Think again.
It took three separate tries to even get through on their support line.
How am I supposed to cope with no Forza Motorsports for that along!? It's inhuman, I tell you.
key_insight: Throwing a billion dollars at a problem means nothing if the actual customer experience remains slow and frustrating.
